1: Carnegie Mellon University was founded in 1900 as Carnegie Technical Schools by Andrew Carnegie.
2: The original purpose of Carnegie Technical Schools was to provide practical skills and education in trades and crafts to working-class individuals in Pittsburgh. Andrew Carnegie envisioned the schools as institutions that would enhance the careers and lives of local residents through two- and three-year certificates in engineering and the arts.
4: In 1967, Carnegie Tech merged with the Mellon Institute to form Carnegie Mellon University.
5: The Mellon Institute of Industrial Research was founded in 1913 by Andrew Mellon and Richard B.
6: The motto of Carnegie Mellon University is "My heart is in the work.
7: Carnegie Mellon University is located in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania
8: Carnegie Mellon University comprises seven colleges and schools. These include the College of Engineering, the School of Computer Science, the Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences, the Mellon College of Science, the Tepper School of Business, Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy, and the College of Fine Arts.
9: The seven colleges and schools are the College of Engineering, the College of Fine Arts, the Dietrich College of Humanities and Social Sciences, the Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy, the Mellon College of Science, the School of Computer Science, and the Tepper School of Business.
10: The School of Computer Science is renowned for its computer science program. It consistently ranks among the top computer science programs in the country and internationally.
11: The business school is the Tepper School of Business
12: Burton Morris is a notable Pop Art artist and Carnegie Mellon alumnus
13: The Tartan is Carnegie Mellon’s student newspaper
14: Carnegie Mellon University’s official mascot is Scotty, a Scottish terrier
15: The official colors of Carnegie Mellon University are cardinal and gray. These colors are reflected in the university’s tartan patterns and mascot attire.
16: The annual event where Carnegie Mellon students build and race buggies is called Sweepstakes, a highlight of Spring Carnival.
17: The Fence is a campus landmark built in 1923 in the middle of the Cut.
18: The College of Fine Arts is housed in several buildings across the campus, including the Frick Fine Arts Building.
19: The main library is Hunt Library
20: Randy Pausch co-created the programming language 'Alice' while he was a professor at Carnegie Mellon University.
21: Randy Pausch’s "Last Lecture," delivered on September 18, 2007, became widely known for its inspirational message on achieving childhood dreams. The lecture was given as Pausch reflected on his life while battling terminal cancer.
22: Andy Bechtolsheim co-founded Sun Microsystems. He earned his M.S
23: Carnegie Mellon’s annual spring carnival is called Spring Carnival
24: Hamerschlag Hall is known for its distinctive cylindrical design on the Carnegie Mellon campus.
25: The Kiltie Band is Carnegie Mellon University’s concert and marching band known for wearing kilts in honor of Andrew Carnegie’s Scottish heritage. It performs at football games, concerts, and other university events.
26: The Robotics Institute is the research institute at Carnegie Mellon focused on robotics. It conducts extensive research in robotics, artificial intelligence, and computer vision.
27: The interdisciplinary program combining technology and the arts is called IDeATe (Integrative Design, Arts, and Technology).
28: Scott Fahlman, a research professor at Carnegie Mellon, is credited with creating the emoticon ':-)' in 1982 on an electronic board to help distinguish jokes from serious posts.
29: Scotty Saturday is a daylong celebration featuring CMU-themed activities, student performances, free snacks, and giveaways.
30: The main administrative hub is the Gates Hillman Center, which houses offices for many academic departments and administrative units.
31: Mobot is an annual robotics competition where student-built autonomous robots race through a slalom course during Spring Carnival. The competition debuted in 1994 and continues as a tradition hosted by the School of Computer Science.
32: Zachary Quinto played Spock in the 'Star Trek' reboot films. He is an alumnus of Carnegie Mellon University.
33: Carnegie Mellon University was named in honor of Andrew Carnegie and the Mellon family.
34: Painting The Fence is a CMU tradition where students paint messages on a campus landmark. Students can paint the fence between midnight and sunrise using only paintbrushes.
35: The Cut is a large grassy area that forms the backbone of CMU’s campus.
36: Allen Newell is known for his contributions to artificial intelligence and cognitive science. He was a pioneer in AI research and developed Soar, an AI system capable of problem-solving and learning.
37: Scotch'n'Soda is a student-run theatre organization at CMU that produces musicals and plays.
38: Andrew Carnegie Hall is named after the university’s founder, Andrew Carnegie
39: The Plaid Parliament of Pwning is a competitive cybersecurity team at CMU that participates in cybersecurity competitions.
40: The School of Art is renowned for its contributions to the arts. Notable alumni include Andy Warhol and Shana Moulton.
41: Mobot is an annual robotics competition where autonomous robots navigate a slalom course marked with gates.
43: Sweepstakes is a tradition where students build and race aerodynamic vehicles called buggies during Spring Carnival. These buggies reach speeds up to 35 mph on a course through Schenley Park.
44: The Mall is a central green space that serves as a gathering place and walkway across campus. It enhances the campus environment and supports community interaction among students, faculty, and staff.
45: Vint Cerf, a CMU alumnus, is known for his work in computer networking and co-developing the ARPANET.
46: Spring Carnival is CMU’s largest annual event, typically held over a four-day weekend in April.
47: The School of Computer Science is housed in the Gates and Hillman Centers. Wean Hall also continues to house some SCS faculty, staff, and students.
48: The Andrew File System (AFS) was developed as part of the Andrew Project in the early 1980s to create a high-speed computer network across the CMU campus. AFS provided a scalable distributed file system designed for large academic and corporate networks.
49: Jaron Lanier is known for his pioneering work in virtual reality. He earned his degrees from CMU and was an early innovator in VR technology.
50: Deep Thought was an early artificial intelligence system developed at CMU
51: The NavLab project was significant for developing autonomous driving technology
52: CMU contributed to speech recognition technology with the development of SPHINX by Kai-Fu Lee, Raj Reddy, and Roberto Bisiani in 1989. SPHINX was the first real-time speech recognition system using AI techniques. This work advanced the capabilities of machines to understand and process human speech.
53: SPHINX, developed by Kai-Fu Lee, Raj Reddy
54: The DARPA Grand Challenge significantly advanced CMU’s robotics program through competitions like the 2007 DARPA Urban Challenge, where Tartan Racing won with Boss, an autonomous vehicle. This victory underscored CMU’s leadership in autonomous vehicle research and earned the team a $2 million prize. The challenge also spurred further innovation in robotics and autonomous systems at CMU.
55: CMU played a significant role in developing self-driving vehicle technology through projects like Navlab 5 and BOSS. Its Robotics Institute has been a leader in autonomous systems research since its founding in 1979. CMU’s work continues to advance self-driving vehicle capabilities.
56: The Iris rover, designed for lunar exploration, was a robotic innovation developed at CMU for planetary exploration.
57: Libratus is an AI system developed by CMU researchers that defeated top poker players in heads-up no-limit Texas Hold'em. CMU is connected to Libratus through its development by computer science faculty members.
58: Pittsburgh Restaurant Week is an annual event featuring dining specials at participating restaurants to highlight Pittsburgh’s culinary scene. It includes winter and summer editions with themed menus focusing on new dishes and seasonal produce, respectively.
59: Bloomfield is Pittsburgh’s Little Italy neighborhood. It hosts Little Italy Days, an annual festival celebrating Italian heritage with food and entertainment.
60: The Pittsburgh Taco Festival is an annual food festival featuring tacos and other Mexican dishes from various vendors. It takes place on September 6, 2025, at The Stacks at 3 Crossings.
61: The Great American Banana Split Celebration in Latrobe celebrates the city’s love for the banana split. This festival includes live music, a banana run, and other activities.
62: The Strip District is known for its unique food vendors and ethnic markets.
63: Wholey’s Fish Market is famous for its fish sandwiches. Situated in the Strip District, it also houses Andy’s Sushi Bar.
64: Gaucho Parrilla Argentina serves Argentinian-inspired sandwiches and wood-fired meat
65: Schwenk’s Restaurant is known for its Pittsburgh Salad topped with fries
66: Pamela's Diner is famous for its super-thin, crepe-style pancakes
67: Schultz’s on Penn is a unique dining spot located in a former church. It features a historic setting with stained glass windows and original architectural details.
68: Sienna Mercato specializes in Italian cuisine
69: Prantl's Bakery is known for its burnt almond torte
70: Pusadee's Garden is known for its elegant Thai cuisine and romantic atmosphere in Lawrenceville.
71: Churn Ice Cream is known for unique flavors like Salty Caramel. Millie's Homemade Ice Cream also offers distinctive treats with local ingredients.
72: Morcilla serves Spanish cuisine in Pittsburgh
73: Espresso A Mano is known for its impressive latte art and fine espresso beverages. They have locations in Squirrel Hill, Dormont, and Lawrenceville.
74: DiAnoia’s Eatery is known for its wide range of pastries, coffee, and Italian pasta dishes.
75: Braddock’s Rebellion offers a farm-to-table dining experience featuring Chef Nicholas Saxon’s modern approach to classic regional fare with support from local farms. ShadoBeni also emphasizes fresh, local ingredients in its Trinidadian cuisine.
76: The Eagle serves homestyle comfort food in Lawrenceville
77: Apteka is famous for its pierogi dishes in Pittsburgh. The restaurant serves both traditional and innovative pierogi fillings.
78: Chengdu Gourmet is a restaurant specializing in Sichuan cuisine in Pittsburgh
79: East Liberty is known for its array of vegan and vegetarian restaurants. Sia's Garden Grown and Essence Café are notable vegan dining options in the neighborhood.
80: Apteka serves Central and Eastern European cuisine with vegan options like pierogi, potato dumplings, stuffed peppers, and schnitzel.
81: Twelve Whiskey Barbecue is known for its extensive whiskey selection in Pittsburgh
82: Noodlehead is a student favorite known for its Thai street-style cuisine
83: Sienna Mercato offers a rooftop beer garden with sweeping views of Pittsburgh’s iconic skyscrapers.
84: täkō serves a blend of Mexican and Japanese cuisines
85: The Pump Handle is known for its brunch offerings and Bloody Mary bar.
86: Dish Osteria and Bar serves Sicilian- and Mediterranean-infused cuisine
87: Fet Fisk is a specialty seafood restaurant in Pittsburgh
88: McKees Rocks American Legion Post 674 was voted the best fish fry in Western Pennsylvania.
89: Iron Born Pizza is unique for its specialty pizzas cooked in a custom-built iron skillet, giving each pie a crispy crust and distinct smoky flavor. Located in the Strip District, it offers a distinctive dining experience with its innovative cooking method.
90: Walnut Street offers upscale shopping and dining experiences with boutiques and high-end restaurants.
91: Visitors can expect shopping and dining options at Waterworks Shopping Plaza
92: Penn Avenue has transformed into a center for arts, dining, and music with gallery showings, live performances, and local vendor events.
93: Wholey's is known for Andy's Sushi Bar
94: Station Square offers dining, carriage rides
95: Market Square is one of Pittsburgh’s most historic public spaces, originally built in 1764.
96: The South Side is known for its vibrant arts scene with diverse shopping and nightlife. Lawrenceville also has a growing arts district with galleries and studios.
97: The Enrico Biscotti Company is known for its macarons in Pittsburgh. Located in the Strip District, it offers a variety of pastries including macarons.
98: The Vandal is known for its specialty burgers and casual dining experience
99: The Market Square Farmers Market operates seasonally from May to October. For year-round shopping, the 2025 Peoples Gas Holiday Market runs from November 22 through December 24.
100: Driftwood Oven is known for its wood-fired pizza
101: The East Pittsburgh Greek Fest focuses on Greek cuisine with events from July 24-26, 2025. It features indoor and outdoor dining areas with authentic Greek dishes and entertainment.
102: Geno's Steaks is a famous Pittsburgh cheesesteak restaurant. Gray's Papaya and other Philadelphia cheesesteak places can also be found in Pittsburgh.
103: The Grand Concourse is known for its dining offerings, featuring multiple course specials with options like maple glazed salmon and gouda stuffed chicken during restaurant week.
104: Visitors can find an authentic Brazilian steakhouse experience at Fogo de Chao Brazilian Steakhouse in Pittsburgh.
105: Mon Aimee Chocolat is known for its handmade confections and imported chocolates. The shop offers local sweets including LUX Artisan Chocolates and Salted Caramels.
106: Tessaro’s is known for its pierogis
107: Music Go Round is known for its large selection of vintage and rare vinyl records.
108: Pittsburgh was named in 1758 by Scottish General John Forbes in honor of British statesman William Pitt, 1st Earl of Chatham. Forbes likely pronounced it similar to "Edinburgh." The city was incorporated as a borough in 1794.
109: Pittsburgh is located in southwestern Pennsylvania at the confluence of the Allegheny and Monongahela rivers, forming the Ohio River. The city sits on the Allegheny Plateau and is characterized by hills and valleys.
110: Pittsburgh is known as "the Steel City" for its historic role in the U.S.
112: A significant event was the capture of Fort Duquesne by British forces in 1758 during the French and Indian War.
113: Pittsburgh was incorporated as a city on March 18, 1816
114: In 1758, General John Forbes named the site Pittsburgh in honor of William Pitt, recognizing its strategic importance. This year marked the expulsion of the French from Fort Duquesne, securing British control of the area. The naming of Pittsburgh in 1758 established the site’s identity and set the stage for its future development.
115: The Allegheny and Monongahela rivers converge to form the Ohio River at Point State Park in Pittsburgh.
116: The Whiskey Rebellion was a tax protest against a federal whiskey tax imposed in 1791.
117: Pittsburgh's geography, with its rivers and access to coal and iron ore deposits, facilitated industrial development. The city’s strategic location along major waterways and rail routes supported transportation and trade. This natural resource wealth and infrastructure attracted heavy industry and spurred economic growth.
118: During the American Civil War, Pittsburgh’s economy was boosted by increased production of iron and armaments. Factories like the Allegheny Arsenal and the Fort Pitt Foundry manufactured weapons and materials critical to the Union war effort. This period marked significant industrial growth, setting the stage for Pittsburgh’s future as a major manufacturing center.
120: The Fort Pitt Blockhouse is Pittsburgh’s oldest architectural landmark, built in 1764 as part of Fort Pitt during the French and Indian War. It is the only remaining structure of the original fort and is owned by the Pittsburgh Chapter of the National Society Daughters of the American Revolution.
121: The H.J. Heinz Company was founded in Pittsburgh in 1869
122: In 1901, Carnegie and Frick merged their companies with others to form United States Steel Corporation, marking a consolidation in Pittsburgh’s steel industry. This merger solidified Pittsburgh’s position as a major steel production center.
123: After World War II, Pittsburgh underwent a major economic transformation. The city initiated cleanup and redevelopment programs to address pollution and diversify its industries. By the 1980s, the steel industry had largely declined due to foreign competition and economic shifts, leading to a focus on technology, healthcare, and services.
124: The Renaissance period, also known as Urban Renewal, began in 1946 with efforts to transform Pittsburgh’s industrial landscape and address environmental issues like air pollution. Major projects included demolition and redevelopment in areas like Gateway Center and the Hill District.
125: The University of Pittsburgh was founded in 1787. It began as the Pittsburgh Academy and evolved through several name changes before becoming the University of Pittsburgh in 1908.
126: In 1964, the Pittsburgh Pirates won the World Series, marking their most recent championship title.
127: Pittsburgh has 446 bridges, more than any other city in the world. This nickname earned it the title "City of Bridges." Around 40 of these bridges cross the three rivers near downtown.
128: Point State Park is a 36-acre public park at the nexus of Pittsburgh’s three rivers.
129: Squirrel Hill is known for its cultural diversity, including a large Jewish population. Other neighborhoods like the Hill District and Homewood also have diverse communities with distinct cultural heritages.
130: The Cathedral of Learning is a 42-story Gothic Revival skyscraper on the University of Pittsburgh’s main campus. It is the tallest educational building in the Western Hemisphere and was completed in 1934.
131: Alcoa is a Pittsburgh-based company known for its aluminum products. Pittsburgh bankers helped finance an aluminum reduction company that became Alcoa.
132: The Homestead Strike was a major labor conflict in 1892 at Carnegie Steel’s Homestead plant.
133: Andy Warhol was born in the neighborhood of Oakland, not North Shore where the Andy Warhol Museum is located. Oakland is known as his birthplace.
134: The Pittsburgh Renaissance Historic District refers to areas transformed by urban renewal projects starting in the 1950s. These initiatives aimed to revitalize downtown Pittsburgh through large-scale demolition and redevelopment.
135: Carnegie Museum of Art is dedicated to contemporary art alongside other historical collections. The Mattress Factory and the Andy Warhol Museum are also contemporary art museums in Pittsburgh.
136: In 1979, the Pittsburgh Pirates won the World Series
137: East Carson Street in the South Side neighborhood is known for its historic Victorian architecture, featuring 15 blocks designated as a National Historic District.
138: Phipps Conservatory and Botanical Gardens is a botanical garden and conservatory in Schenley Park, Pittsburgh.
139: Acrisure Stadium is the home stadium of the Pittsburgh Steelers. It is located in the North Shore neighborhood of Pittsburgh.
140: The Carnegie Science Center is a hands-on science museum featuring interactive exhibits, Buhl Planetarium, and the Miniature Railroad & Village. It was added to Carnegie Museums of Pittsburgh in 1991 through a partnership with the Buhl Foundation.
141: Andrew Carnegie was a Scottish-American industrialist and philanthropist who played a major role in Pittsburgh’s steel industry. He founded the Carnegie Steel Company and later sold it to form U.S. Steel.
142: The South Side is known for its vibrant arts scene with diverse shopping and nightlife. Lawrenceville also has a growing arts district with galleries and studios.
143: Pittsburgh’s inclines are unique for their steep 30-degree ascent and historic design
144: The Nationality Rooms program is a series of decorated classrooms within the University of Pittsburgh’s Cathedral of Learning, each representing a different immigrant group that contributed to Pittsburgh’s history. The program began in 1926 with rooms designed and funded by community groups, often with assistance from foreign governments.
145: The longest bridge in downtown Pittsburgh is the Fort Pitt Bridge, which carries I-376 across the Ohio River.
146: The Smithfield Street Bridge is significant as it is the oldest river crossing in Pittsburgh, opening in 1883. It was the world’s first lenticular truss bridge and is designated as both a Historic Civil Engineering Landmark and a National Historic Landmark.
147: Primanti Brothers sandwiches are a famous Pittsburgh specialty. These sandwiches feature meat, cheese, French fries, coleslaw, and tomato slices between two slices of Italian bread.
148: The Allegheny County Courthouse houses the Court of Common Pleas and Magisterial District Courts, which handle major trials and minor criminal and civil cases respectively.
149: Pittsburgh's population as of the 2020 census is 302,971
150: Pittsburgh’s current economy is driven by healthcare, education, technology
151: Carnegie Mellon University is known for robotics research through its Robotics Institute, one of the world’s leading robotics research facilities. Established in 1979, the institute conducts extensive research in areas like medical and space robotics.
152: The Golden Triangle is Downtown Pittsburgh, located at the confluence of the Allegheny and Monongahela Rivers.
153: The Duquesne University Red Masquers is the oldest continuously producing theater company in Pennsylvania, founded in 1912. However, the oldest operating theater building in Pittsburgh may be the one built in 1812.
154: The Carnegie Museums are located in Oakland, a neighborhood in Pittsburgh. The Carnegie Museum of Art and the Carnegie Museum of Natural History are part of the Carnegie Institute and Library complex in Oakland.
155: The Pittsburgh Zoo & Aquarium is known for its year-round animal exhibits and educational experiences.
156: The Roberto Clemente Bridge was originally built in 1927 as the third Sixth Street Bridge.
157: The Strip District is famous for its ethnic food markets and eateries
